objective.statement: Inject durable repo-memory swarm messages into Codex, Claude Code, Kimi Code, GitHub Copilot CLI, and Cursor through their native lifecycle hooks.
objective.measure: One shared hook adapter polls repo_memory over MCP, emits only client-native context output, delays acknowledgement until the next lifecycle boundary, preserves the filesystem bus as a migration fallback, and never treats messaging as authority.

need.statement: Inject durable repo-memory swarm messages into Codex, Claude Code, Kimi Code, GitHub Copilot CLI, and Cursor through their native lifecycle hooks.
stakeholders: Agent clients on the devbox that share the CentralCloud MCP gateway and repo-memory swarm bus.
need.baseline: Claude Code and Kimi Code invoke one shared hook, but it only polls the host-local /tmp JSON bus. Codex installs the script with an empty hooks.json, while Copilot CLI and Cursor have no repo-memory delivery hook. All five clients already receive the CentralCloud MCP gateway configuration.
need.consequence_of_no_action: Durable PostgreSQL-backed messages remain invisible unless an agent remembers to call repo-memory manually, and cross-host coordination continues to depend on ephemeral local files.
assumptions: The devbox can reach http://mcp-gateway.svc/mcp without embedding a downstream repo-memory credential.
alternatives_considered: Give every client a separate transport implementation

purpose: Inject durable repo-memory swarm messages into Codex, Claude Code, Kimi Code, GitHub Copilot CLI, and Cursor through their native lifecycle hooks.
consumer: Agent clients on the devbox that share the CentralCloud MCP gateway and repo-memory swarm bus.
contract: One shared hook adapter polls repo_memory over MCP, emits only client-native context output, delays acknowledgement until the next lifecycle boundary, preserves the filesystem bus as a migration fallback, and never treats messaging as authority.
failure_boundary: Gateway, protocol, malformed-message, or client-format failures fail open without acknowledging the durable message. The existing filesystem adapter remains available during migration, and no hook receives database credentials.

evidence.description: Node contract tests prove MCP initialization/calls, client-native output, next-boundary acknowledgements, idempotent availability, and filesystem fallback; Home Manager evaluation proves durable installation.

falsifier.claim: The change delivers the promised contract.

executable_check.description: Run the contract test; any failure falsifies the claim.

non_goals: Claiming lifecycle hooks wake idle clients
Removing the local filesystem bus in this slice
Letting messages authorize edits, VCS, deployments, secrets, or completion
Adding direct PostgreSQL access to clients
invariants: Messages are acknowledged only at a later observed client boundary
One shared adapter owns transport and rendering
Client output follows each native hook schema
No secret is written to hook configuration or output

assumptions.statement: Cursor can inject swarm context only at sessionStart; mid-session delivery remains MCP polling by the agent until Cursor exposes a documented prompt-context hook.
assumptions.falsifier: Current Cursor hook documentation or a live hook probe demonstrates supported beforeSubmitPrompt context injection.

plane_constraints: Repo-memory owns durable messages; CentralCloud MCP gateway owns downstream routing; Home Manager owns user-level client hook configuration.
